title: Electric Power Steering (EPS) Structure Description - Torque Sensor

blocks.text: Electric Power Steering (EPS) Structure Description - Torque Sensor
The torque sensor uses a non-contact variable inductance (self-induced voltage) method to detect steering torque and road surface reaction force.
The torque sensor consists of an input shaft, worm wheel shaft, torsion bar, sensor sleeve integrated into the input shaft, coil 1, and coil 2.

The worm wheel shaft is magnetic and the sensor part is splined with a vertical groove. The coil on the housing side maintains a constant magnetic field density with the protruding end of the worm wheel shaft across the sensor sleeve. The sensor sleeve has a sleeve window, and changing the position of the sleeve window changes the magnetic field density between the coil and the worm wheel shaft.

When the steering wheel is operated, the torsion bar is twisted between the worm wheel shaft on the steering side and the pinion shaft on the road surface reaction side. In response to this twisting, the position of the tip of the worm wheel shaft projection and the sleeve window shifts, causing a phase shift and a change in the magnetic field line density between the coil and the worm wheel shaft (the magnetic field line density is higher when the tip of the worm wheel shaft projection is in the sleeve window). This change in magnetic field line density changes the inductance (self-induced voltage) of the coil. The steering torque is proportional to this inductance.

When steering wheel operation is performed, the magnetic flux density on one coil side increases and the magnetic flux density on the other coil side decreases. The steering direction is determined by generating this difference as a waveform, and the steering torque is determined from the magnitude of the difference.
The detection circuit consists of two systems: a main signal circuit for outputting sensor values to the system and a sub-signal circuit for fail-safe control.

The torque sensor detects the change in magnetic flux density that occurs in coil 1 and coil 2 as a waveform and amplifies it in the torque sensor circuit to generate the main signal and sub-signal. When steering torque occurs with the steering wheel turning to the right, the voltage of the main signal increases and the voltage of the sub-signal decreases. The opposite is true for steering torque at left steering of the steering wheel. When no steering torque is generated, both the main and sub signals are 2.5 V. The amount of this voltage change is output to the control unit as a steering signal.
If the EPS control unit determines that there is a difference in the output signals (absolute values of voltages), the EPS control unit performs fail-safe control.
